clean_story:
Surface irregularities. Sensory language.

The word texture comes from Latin textura—"weaving"—because even the smoothest surface is a fabric of tiny peaks and valleys. A brick wall isn’t just rough; its ridges are 0.5 to 3 millimeters high, designed to grip mortar like Velcro. A strawberry’s seeds aren’t bumps but achenes, each a minuscule fruit with a wood-like shell, packed at 200 per square centimeter. Texture deceives: Silk feels cool because its fibers wick heat 1.5 times faster than cotton, though both sit at room temperature.

Chefs exploit this. A crisp potato chip’s snap comes from starch crystals fracturing at 3–5% moisture—any wetter, and it bends like a soggy leaf. Artists reverse-engineer it: Van Gogh’s Starry Night swirls aren’t random; his brushstrokes average 0.3 millimeters thick, thick enough to cast shadows that trick eyes into seeing depth. Even "smooth" is a lie: A polished granite countertop, flat to fingertips, has microscopic pits where feldspar crystals eroded—visible only under 100x magnification.

Three related subjects are friction’s hidden physics, how tongues detect roughness, and why sand feels different wet.
